full_text:
Heatwaves are no longer just uncomfortable—they can be dangerous. From dehydration to heat stroke, rising temperatures are putting millions at risk. - Extreme heat can cause dehydration, exhaustion, and heat stroke
- Elderly, young kids, and the ill face the greatest risk
- Hydrate, limit exertion, and wear light clothing for safety
Did our AI summary help?
Abnormally high temperature conditions are becoming common and intense over several parts of India as we move towards hotter months. As average global temperatures rise, people are soon going to experience longer periods of heat that are uncomfortable and, in some cases, dangerous. Although high temperatures are commonly linked to summer, extended time in extreme heat can have a serious impact on overall health. Long exposure to extreme heat can interfere with the body’s natural cooling mechanism, causing dehydration, exhaustion and serious ailments.
What Happens to the Body During Extreme Heat
Story continues below Advertisement
According to Dr Aravind Badiger, Technical Director, BDR Pharmaceuticals, the body‘s internal temperature is maintained by thermoregulation at approximately 37 degrees (98.6 degrees F). When the temperature increases, blood vessels near the skin will dilate and sweat glands will secrete sweat and as the sweat evaporates it helps release heat and cools the body.
“However, in extremely high temperatures or humidity, this heat may not evaporate as freely and the body‘s core temperature may rise quickly putting strain on vital organs such as the heart, brain and kidneys,” says the doctor.

Common Heat-Related Illnesses
There are a number of health conditions associated with heatwaves. The first one that may occur is heat cramps. These generally are the first symptom that is noticed and are painful spasms in the muscle tissue due to loss of the body‘s salt and water through profuse sweating.
The next step is Heat exhaustion, this is caused by the loss of a large amount of salt and water by the body and has symptoms of lethargy, dizziness, nausea and profuse sweating. If heat exhaustion is left without treatment, it can lead to heat stroke.
Story continues below Advertisement
Who Is Most Vulnerable
Doctor Badiger says some age groups are particularly susceptible to getting ill from the effects of heat, for example elderly and the very young who are less capable of heat regulation. People who already have existing health conditions such as heart disease, diabetes or kidney problems are more at risk in a heatwave.
Workers who are exposed to long hours of outdoor work, sportsmen, and people who live in densely populated urban centers are often exposed to high temperatures over long periods of time.
“The urban heat island effect is also experienced in urban centers, whereby structures and surfaces tend to collect and retain heat, resulting in temperatures being significantly higher in urban centers than in other areas,” says the doctor.
Warning Signs That Should Not Be Ignored
The symptoms of heat-related illness at the early stage are loss of sweat, dizziness, headache, fatigue, muscle cramps and nausea. If not taken care of, in the later stages it can cause confusion, unconsciousness, increase in pulse rate and respiration body organs damaged in permanent and eventually cause death.

How to Stay Protected During Heatwaves
There are some simple, basic steps to take that will prevent heat-related illnesses. Keep oneself well hydrated with plenty of water as it will prevent dehydration and enable the body‘s cooling system. Avoid heavy activities such as exercise during the hottest parts of the day (from about 11 am to 4 pm) in the heat. Wearing loose comfortable light-coloured clothing, using hats and umbrellas can help protect against direct sunlight.
FAQs on Heatwaves and Protection
1. What is a heatwave, and why is it dangerous?
A heatwave is a period of abnormally high temperatures that lasts for several days and is much hotter than the usual weather for that area. It is dangerous because long exposure to extreme heat can interfere with the body’s natural cooling mechanism, leading to dehydration, exhaustion, heat cramps, heat exhaustion and, if not treated, heat stroke which can damage vital organs and even cause death.
2. Who is most at risk during a heatwave?
Elderly people, very young children and people with existing health conditions such as heart disease, diabetes or kidney problems are more vulnerable because they are less capable of regulating body temperature. Workers who spend long hours outdoors, sportsmen and people living in densely populated urban centres are also at higher risk due to prolonged exposure to high temperatures and the urban heat island effect.
3. What are the early warning signs of heat-related illness?
Early warning signs include loss of sweat, dizziness, headache, fatigue, muscle cramps and nausea. These symptoms indicate that the body is struggling to cool itself and may be losing too much salt and water through sweating. These signs should not be ignored, as they can progress to more serious symptoms if not addressed.
4. How can I protect myself during a heatwave?
To stay protected, keep yourself well hydrated with plenty of water to prevent dehydration and support the body’s cooling system. Avoid heavy activities such as exercise during the hottest parts of the day, typically from about 11 am to 4 pm. Wear loose, comfortable, light-coloured clothing and use hats and umbrellas to protect against direct sunlight.
5. When should I seek medical help for heat-related problems?
You should seek medical help immediately if early symptoms like dizziness, headache, fatigue or muscle cramps worsen or if more serious signs appear, such as confusion, unconsciousness, a rapid pulse rate, difficulty in breathing or any indication that body organs may be affected. Timely medical attention can prevent permanent damage and potentially save a life.
